edge: GFPose is a framework that models 3D human poses and incorporates pose priors implicitly during the denoising process.
GFPose at its core involves a time-dependent score network that estimates the gradient on each body joint.
GFPose outperforms existing SOTAs by 20% on Human3.6M dataset as a multi-hypothesis pose estimator.
GFPose achieves comparable results to deterministic SOTAs, even with a vanilla backbone, as a single-hypothesis pose estimator.

Condition masking strategy introduces randomly masked task conditions to handle various pose-related tasks in a unified learning-based framework.

Hierarchical condition masking strategy involves randomly masking out the task condition c by sampling masks from different levels of randomness, enabling the model to build spatial relations between body joints and parts.
The candidate masks cover different levels of randomness including human level, body part level, and joint level to build spatial relations between different body joints and parts.

To handle different applications and enhance the versatility, a hierarchical masking strategy is designed to randomly mask the task condition c while training sθ(x, t
The mask is applied to the task condition while training to control the information flow and features learned by the model.
A 3-level mask hierarchy is designed to handle human-pose-related tasks, consisting of Mhuman, Mpart, and Mjoint.
Mhuman determines if a condition is fully masked out with a certain probability, impacting the pose prior pdata(x
Mpart decides whether each human body part should be masked out with a specific probability, aiding in pose completion by handling occlusions.
Mjoint determines if each human joint should be masked out independently with a certain probability, assisting in pose completion by addressing occluded joints.
The model is augmented with the mask hierarchy during training to improve performance in human-pose-related tasks.

Comparisons are made between different models based on minMPJPE under Protocol #1 in the Ablation Study.
The ablation study focuses on different factors that affect the performance of the model on the 3D pose estimation task, including the number of sampling steps, depth, and width of the network.
The ablation study shows that more sampling steps do not necessarily improve the reconstruction accuracy but greatly reduce the inference speed.
GFPose is introduced as a versatile framework to model the 3D human pose prior via denoising score matching.
A condition masking strategy is proposed to enhance the versatility of GFPose by incorporating pose prior and task-specific conditions into gradient fields for various applications.
